timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Ezra Reads the Law

heb.paragraph: Eighty-four names seal the document: Nehemiah first, then priests, Levites, and lay leaders. The naming is the commitment. Each signature binds a family.

panels.ctx: The list serves a legal function: these are the guarantors. If the covenant is broken, these families bear responsibility. Public naming creates public accountability.

cross.note: Joshua’s covenant with witnesses — the same legal pattern.

notes.note: Nehemiah signs first as governor. Political authority backs religious commitment. Church and state align in covenant purpose.
Twenty-one priests, seventeen Levites, forty-four lay leaders. The three tiers of community leadership are all represented. No class exempts itself.
Calvin: "The governor signed first. Leaders must lead in commitment, not merely in command."

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes "on the sealed document" (ʿal-heḥātûm) indicates a clay-sealed or wax-sealed legal instrument.
Williamson compares the signatory list with names from chapters 3 and 12, finding significant overlap. The same families who built the wall now seal the covenant.
Kidner: "Eighty-four signatures. Each name is a promise. The covenant is not abstract but personal — bound to named individuals who can be held accountable."

heb.paragraph: "We will not neglect the house of our God" (v.39). The covenant’s final sentence and its summary. Everything else — intermarriage, Sabbath, tithes, wood offering — serves this one principle.

panels.ctx: The commitments are specific, not vague: no intermarriage (v.30), Sabbath observance including refusing to buy from foreigners on the Sabbath (v.31), temple tax (v.32), wood offering (v.34), firstfruits (vv.35–37), tithes (vv.37–38). Each addresses a failure documented earlier in the book. The covenant is not aspirational but corrective.

cross.note: The intermarriage prohibition the covenant reaffirms.
The Sabbath and sabbatical year the covenant revives.

notes.note: No intermarriage — the issue from Ezra 9–10 is formally prohibited. The community draws a line it failed to hold before.
Sabbath enforcement: "We will not buy from foreign merchants on the Sabbath." Economic activity as covenant faithfulness. What you buy and when you buy it is a spiritual act.
A self-imposed temple tax of one-third shekel. The Torah prescribes half a shekel (Exod 30:13); the community adjusts downward because of poverty. Faithfulness within means.
"We will not neglect the house of our God." The covenant’s last word. Everything else flows from and toward this: the temple as the centre of covenant life.
Calvin: "We will not neglect. The simplest and most necessary of all vows. Neglect, not rebellion, is the church’s constant danger."

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET discusses the one-third shekel as an adaptation from the Exod 30:13 half-shekel, likely reflecting economic hardship in the post-exilic community.

Williamson notes the Sabbath commercial prohibition goes beyond Torah’s work-rest framework. The community is legislating against economic pressure to trade on the Sabbath — a specific post-exilic challenge.
Kidner: "One-third shekel instead of half. They were too poor for the full amount but too faithful to give nothing. Grace adjusts the standard to the capacity."
Kidner: "The final sentence is the covenant in miniature. Not neglect. The opposite of faithfulness is not hostility but inattention."

ppl.name: Nehemiah
ppl.text: Nehemiah is a key figure in The Sealed Covenant.

ppl.name: Ezra
ppl.text: No intermarriage — the issue from Ezra 9–10 is formally prohibited…

Zedekiah,ESV“On the seals are the names of Nehemiah the governor, the son of Hacaliah, Zedekiah,

rec.text: "Nehemiah signs first as governor. Political authority backs religious commitment. Church and state align in covenant purpose."

rows.label: The Names — 10:1–27
rows.text: Eighty-four signatures. Each name a promise, each promise binding.

rows.label: The Commitments — 10:28–39
rows.text: Specific, corrective, within means. "We will not neglect the house of our God."

"We will not neglect the house of our God" (v.39). The covenant’s final sentence and its summary. Everything else — intermarriage, Sabbath, tithes, wood offering — serves this one principle.

The Sabbath and sabbatical year the covenant revives.

themes.note: Covenant at 10 again: the chapter IS the covenant. Holiness is high — every commitment targets boundary-keeping.
